title: How to Deal with Spiritual Confusion About Your Values
meta_description: Struggling with shifting values after a spiritual awakening? This article helps you navigate confusion without judgment. Learn why disorientation means growth, not failure.
og_title: How to Deal with Spiritual Confusion About Your Values
og_description: Struggling with shifting values after a spiritual awakening? This article helps you navigate confusion without judgment. Learn why disorientation means growth, not failure.
body:
You're Not Lost. You're Just in the Thick of It.

Let me guess. You used to know exactly what you believed. You had a moral compass that pointed north without question. Your values were clear - family, hard work, honesty, maybe faith or community. Then something shifted. A spiritual awakening, a loss, a crisis, or just a quiet moment where you realized the old rules don't fit anymore. And now you're standing in the middle of your own life, feeling like a stranger to yourself. You're asking: What do I actually value? And why does it feel so confusing?

First, take a breath. You're not broken. You're not doing this wrong. Spiritual confusion about your values is not a sign that you've lost your way - it's a sign that you're growing. And growth, real growth, always comes with a period of disorientation. Think of it like this: you're shedding a skin that's too tight. The old one served you, but now it's constricting. The new one hasn't fully formed yet. So you're in between. And that in-between space? It's the most honest place you can be.

I've been there. I've sat on my couch at 2 AM, questioning everything I built my life on. I've cried over things I used to defend with my whole chest. And I've learned that this confusion isn't an enemy to fight - it's a teacher to listen to. So let's walk through this together. No fluff. No spiritual bypassing. Just a direct, grounded look at how to deal with spiritual confusion about your values.

Why Your Values Feel Like They're Falling Apart

Here's the thing: your values are not static. They're not carved in stone. They're more like a living map that shifts as you do. When you go through a spiritual awakening - and I mean a real one, not just a weekend retreat where you bought some crystals - your entire approach for understanding reality gets rewritten. The ground you stood on turns out to be sand. And that's terrifying and freeing at the same time.

The confusion usually hits because your old values were inherited. You got them from your parents, your culture, your religion, your social circle. They were handed to you like a set of rules: this is good, this is bad, this is what a successful person looks like, this is what a moral person does. And for a while, those rules worked. They gave you structure. They helped you work through the world without having to think too hard about every choice.

But then you started waking up. Maybe you read something that cracked open a new perspective. Maybe you had an experience that showed you there's more to life than the script you were given. Maybe you just got tired of living for other people's approval. And suddenly, those inherited values don't feel true anymore. They feel hollow. Like you're wearing someone else's clothes.

"The confusion you feel is not a sign that you've lost your way. It's a sign that your soul is demanding a more honest map. The old one was borrowed. The new one has to be built by you, from the ground up."

Does that land? Because this is the core of it. Your confusion isn't a problem to solve. It's a signal that you're ready to stop living on autopilot. And that's a brave thing, even when it feels like a mess.

The Difference Between Inherited Values and Chosen Values

Let's get specific. Inherited values are the ones you absorbed without question. They sound like: "I should be successful," "I should put family first," "I should be a good person," "I should be spiritual," "I should be financially stable." Notice the "should." That's the key. Inherited values are driven by obligation, fear, or expectation. They come with a sense of pressure. You follow them because you're supposed to, not because they light you up.

Chosen values, on the other hand, are the ones you've tested against your own experience. They sound like: "I value honesty because I've seen how lies poison relationships," "I value creativity because it's the only time I feel alive," "I value solitude because it's where I find my center." Chosen values come from the inside out. They're not borrowed. They're earned through living, through failing, through paying attention to what actually makes you feel whole.

Right now, you're probably holding both sets of values at the same time, and they're fighting each other. That's the confusion. Your inherited values are screaming, "You need to be this way!" And your emerging chosen values are whispering, "But what if you tried this instead?" The whisper is quieter, but it's more true. The question is: how do you start listening to it?

How to Untangle Your Values Without Losing Your Mind

1. Get Honest About What You're Actually Doing

Start with observation, not judgment. For one week, just watch yourself. Notice when you feel a sense of resentment, fatigue, or resistance around something you "should" do. That's your clue. Resentment is a sign that you're living by a value that isn't really yours. It's like a muscle cramp from holding a position you're not meant to hold.

Write it down. Keep a simple list. "I feel resentful when I go to church because I don't believe what they're saying anymore." "I feel drained when I chase a promotion I don't even want." "I feel empty when I scroll social media comparing myself to people who live differently." Don't judge these feelings. Just notice them. They're data points. They're telling you where your inherited values are pinching.

2. Ask Yourself the Hard Questions

Once you've gathered some data, sit down with it. Ask yourself these questions, and don't settle for easy answers:

  • If no one was watching, what would I do with my time?
  • What makes me feel more alive, not just more productive?
  • What have I been doing because I'm afraid of disappointing someone?
  • What would I regret not honoring at the end of my life?
  • What feels true in my gut, even if it doesn't make logical sense?

These questions are not meant to give you instant clarity. They're meant to open a door. You might not have answers right away, and that's okay. The act of asking them is already shifting something. You're telling your subconscious, "I'm ready to listen." And it will start feeding you pieces of truth, sometimes in dreams, sometimes in sudden insights, sometimes in quiet moments when you're not trying.

3. Give Yourself Permission to Change Your Mind

This is the hard one. Most of us have this idea that our values should be consistent. That if we believed something two years ago, we should still believe it now. But that's not how growth works. Growth means your values evolve. What served you at 25 might not serve you at 40. What felt essential in one chapter of your life might feel irrelevant in the next.

And here's the thing: changing your values doesn't make you a hypocrite. It makes you human. It means you're paying attention. It means you're not clinging to a dead version of yourself just to avoid looking confused. So give yourself that permission. Say it out loud: "I am allowed to change my mind about what matters to me." Say it until you believe it.

Practical Tools for Navigating the Fog

Keep a Values Journal

This doesn't have to be fancy. A notebook, a note on your phone, whatever works. Every day, write down one thing that felt aligned with your emerging values and one thing that felt out of alignment. Don't overthink it. Just track it. Over time, you'll start to see patterns. You'll notice that certain activities, people, or environments leave you feeling energized and clear. Others leave you feeling drained and off. Those are your clues.

If you want a structured guide to help you through this process, I recommend checking out The Gifts of Imperfection by Brené Brown. It's a grounded, no-nonsense look at how to live by your own values, not someone else's. See on Amazon (paid link)

Create Space for Silence

You can't hear your own voice if you're constantly drowning it out with noise. Social media, news, podcasts, other people's opinions - they all crowd out the quiet whisper of your own knowing. I'm not saying you need to meditate for an hour a day. But try carving out 10 minutes of silence. No phone. No music. No distractions. Just you and your breath. Sit with the discomfort of not knowing. Let the confusion be there without trying to fix it. You'd be surprised what surfaces when you stop trying to control everything.

Test Your Values in Small Ways

You don't have to overhaul your entire life overnight. That's a recipe for panic. Instead, test your emerging values in low-stakes situations. If you think you value creativity more than stability, try saying no to one extra work commitment and spending that time on a creative project. If you think you value authenticity over politeness, try being honest about your feelings in a conversation where you'd normally people-please. See how it feels. Does it light you up? Does it make you anxious? Both are valid. You're gathering information.

When Spiritual Confusion Shows Up as Guilt

One of the hardest parts of this process is the guilt. You might feel guilty for letting go of values that were handed down by people you love. You might feel guilty for wanting something different than what your family or community expects. You might feel guilty for not being "consistent" or "stable." That guilt is real, and it's not something to ignore.

But here's what I want you to understand: guilt is often a sign that you're breaking an old agreement. An agreement you made when you were younger, more dependent, more afraid. That agreement served you then. It kept you safe. But it might not be serving you now. You're allowed to renegotiate. You're allowed to say, "I see that value, I respect where it came from, and I'm choosing to set it down now because it no longer fits."

Let the guilt be there. Don't fight it. But don't let it drive the bus either. Just notice it, thank it for trying to protect you, and keep moving toward what feels true.

How to Know When You're on the Right Track

You might be wondering: how do I know if I'm actually growing or just running away? It's a fair question. Here's the test: growth feels expansive, even when it's uncomfortable. Running away feels contractive. Growth opens up possibilities - you feel more alive, more curious, more connected to yourself, even when you're scared. Running away closes things down - you feel smaller, more defensive, more numb.

Pay attention to your body. Your nervous system knows the difference. When you're aligned with a chosen value, you might feel a sense of warmth in your chest, a relaxation in your shoulders, a feeling of "yes" even if the situation is hard. When you're acting from an inherited value that doesn't fit, you'll feel tightness, tension, a sense of "should" that drains your energy. Trust that physical knowing. It's smarter than your thinking mind.

For a deeper exploration of how to trust your own inner guidance, I highly recommend The Untethered Soul by Michael A. Singer. It's a direct, practical guide to getting out of your own way and listening to what's actually true. See on Amazon (paid link)

You're Not Alone in This

I want you to hear this clearly: spiritual confusion about your values is not a defect. It's not a sign that you're doing spirituality wrong. It's a sign that you're doing it right. Because real spirituality isn't about having all the answers. It's about being willing to ask the questions. It's about being brave enough to sit in the not-knowing and trust that clarity will come when it's ready.

You're not the first person to feel this way, and you won't be the last. Every human who has ever done deep inner work has gone through this phase. It's the dark night of the soul for your values. And like any dark night, it's not permanent. The dawn comes. But you can't rush it. You can only be present for it.

If you want a practical workbook to guide you through this process, The Desire Map by Danielle LaPorte is a fantastic resource. It helps you get clear on how you want to feel, which is a much more honest foundation for values than any external rulebook. See on Amazon (paid link)

The Values That Will Never Fail You

As you go through this process, you might discover that some values are more foundational than others. These are the ones that hold up no matter what. They're not tied to a specific religion, culture, or life stage. They're universal in the sense that they connect you to your deepest self. Things like:

  • Authenticity - the willingness to be real, even when it's messy
  • Compassion - the choice to be kind, starting with yourself
  • Curiosity - the openness to keep learning and growing
  • Courage - the strength to act on what you know, even when it's scary
  • Connection - the recognition that you're part of something larger

These values are like anchor points. When everything else feels shaky, you can come back to them. They're not about what you should do. They're about how you want to be. And that's the shift that makes all the difference.

For a beautiful exploration of how to live from these core values, check out Rising Strong by Brené Brown. It's all about what happens when you fall, get back up, and rebuild your life on more honest ground. See on Amazon (paid link)

A Final Word

You're not confused because you're broken. You're confused because you're waking up. And waking up is disorienting. It's like being in a dream and suddenly realizing you're dreaming - the whole world shifts, and you have to reorient yourself to a new reality. That's what's happening to you right now. Your old values were part of a dream you used to believe was real. Now you're seeing through it. And the new reality? It's not fully formed yet. But it will be.

Trust the process. Trust yourself. And when the confusion feels overwhelming, come back to this simple truth: you are not here to live someone else's life. You are here to live your own. And that means discovering your own values, one honest moment at a time.

If you're ready to go deeper into this work, I've created a free assessment to help you identify where you are in your spiritual awakening and what your next steps might be. You can take it here or check out our spiritual awakening quiz to get more clarity on your unique path.
